metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC bullet 1 into verifiable end-states (e.g., 'A tracking issue lists all child stories and each is closed with a merged PR') and remove 'plan-reviewed' as an AC (it is a process gate, not a repo-observable outcome).
findings.finding: AC bullet 1 is an epic-of-epics pattern: 'created, plan-reviewed, AND closed' spans multiple separate sessions/reviews by definition and is not a single in-session completable outcome. Additionally, 'plan-reviewed' is a process milestone (a human approval step), not a repo-observable artifact, making it impossible to verify deterministically via CI or a command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 1: 'All child stories are created, plan-reviewed, and closed.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: 'A tracking checklist in this issue (or a linked milestone) lists every child story; each child story's PR is merged and its issue is closed — verifiable via the milestone's closed-issue count reaching N.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add behavioral tests for sync branch selection and lock stale-owner/reclamation within 25aa's scope, or explicitly descope those two targets and note they require additional stories before mutation shards are meaningful.
findings.finding: Child 25aa-1df0's rationale states mutation shards are needed 'once behavioral tests for those paths exist (S1, S3, S4),' specifically targeting 'sync branch selection, compaction fold/rollback decisions, push policy/retry classification, lock stale-owner/reclamation.' Of these four targets, only compaction fold/rollback (4001) and push retry (a263 touches push, but only the parent-exit path) and remote-append (1fdc) have sibling behavioral tests. Sync branch selection and lock stale-owner/reclamation have NO sibling that adds behavioral tests for them — 25aa's mutation shards would cover decision logic with no new behavioral test backing, leaving the 'shards target near-pure helpers covered by behavioral tests' rationale partially unmet.
findings.suggested_fix: In 25aa's scope, scope down to compaction fold/rollback and push retry classification (covered by sibling behavioral tests), and add a note that sync and lock shards are deferred until dedicated behavioral stories exist.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the compaction rollback handles partial reverse-rename failure: define whether the SNAPSHOT should be left in place (allowing the SNAPSHOT-present skip to tolerate residual .retired files), or whether a recovery sentinel (analogous to `.snapshot-rebuild.bak` in `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`) should be written so that fsck can detect and remediate the inconsistent state.
findings.finding: The compaction rollback fix ('no longer deletes the snapshot after an incomplete rollback') addresses the outer forward-rename OSError path but leaves a correctness gap in the inner reverse-rename failure sub-case. In compact.py lines 235–241, each reverse os.rename is wrapped in its own try/except that logs and continues — so if a reverse rename fails partway, the ticket ends up with some sources still .retired, others restored, and then (under the current code) no SNAPSHOT. Leaving the SNAPSHOT in place (the implied fix) makes the ticket readable but creates a state where the SNAPSHOT's source_event_uuids are inconsistent with the actually-retired files. The idempotent re-compact short-circuit (SNAPSHOT-present at entry) would then skip re-compaction permanently, freezing the ticket in this inconsistent state. The plan does not specify how the proposed mechanism handles this second-order failure, so an implementer has no guidance on whether the SNAPSHOT-present skip is safe or whether a re-compact should be forced.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Compaction rollback no longer deletes the snapshot after an incomplete rollback.' / src/rebar/_commands/compact.py lines 235–246
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the same `.snapshot-rebuild.bak` sentinel pattern already present in `rebuild_snapshot_from_full_log`: write a sentinel before any rename, and remove it only after a clean round-trip. If the sentinel is present on a subsequent compact/fsck pass, trigger a full rebuild. This makes the 'all-or-none' invariant hold without requiring the reverse-rename loop to be itself atomic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequencing) for the whitespace-title rejection change, and document whether existing stored tickets with whitespace-only titles require a migration.
findings.finding: The whitespace-only title rejection is a breaking behavioral change to the public CLI/library/MCP facades — callers currently passing whitespace strings receive success; after the fix they receive errors. The plan acknowledges the policy decision but provides no expand-contract sequence, no deprecation window, and no back-out plan if the change causes unexpected downstream breakage.
findings.location: Policy decisions section; AC: 'Whitespace-only titles are rejected across CLI/library/MCP facades.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan section: (1) audit stored data for whitespace-only titles and decide on migration or grandfather behavior; (2) release the validation behind a config flag or as a two-phase expand-contract (warn, then error) if any external consumer may be affected; (3) document the back-out procedure (revert the guard, re-deploy) in case of incident.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or child-story task to update user-facing documentation (CLI help text, API reference, and/or CHANGELOG) to document the new whitespace-title rejection behavior.
findings.finding: The new whitespace-only title rejection contract — a user-visible validation rule across CLI, library, and MCP facades — has no corresponding documentation update planned (e.g., CLI help text, API reference, or a changelog entry). The plan only schedules fixes to three named stale-content docs but omits documenting the new behavioral contract.
findings.location: AC: 'Whitespace-only titles are rejected across CLI/library/MCP facades.'; Success Criteria; no doc-update AC for this new validation rule.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: 'CLI --help / API docstring for the title parameter notes that blank or whitespace-only titles are rejected, and a CHANGELOG entry records the new validation rule.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `depends_on: [4001-185c]` (or equivalent ordering statement) to child 44b7-2f9e so it is scheduled after the compact.py rollback fix lands.
findings.finding: Child 44b7-2f9e (SIGKILL recovery test) must run against the post-4001 rollback fix to produce meaningful results: the test asserts 'snapshot is NOT deleted' and 'fsck repairs the state,' but the current compact.py unconditionally deletes the snapshot on rollback failure. If 44b7 lands before 4001, the test either fails (proving nothing about the desired contract) or must be written to tolerate the pre-fix behavior, making it a weaker assertion. No depends_on or ordering statement is documented in 44b7.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'This story must land after 4001-185c (rollback fix) because the SIGKILL-recovery assertions presuppose the corrected rollback behavior (snapshot retained on partial rollback).' to 44b7's Scope or Why section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly coordinate 4001 and 44b7 on their shared `compact.py` changes: either note the failpoint insertion point in 44b7's scope relative to 4001's restructured rollback, or require 44b7 to land after 4001.
findings.finding: Children 4001-185c and 44b7-2f9e both modify `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py` without cross-referencing each other. 4001 restructures the rollback branch; 44b7 adds a test-only failpoint hook 'guarded so it is inert in production' to the same file. Neither child names the other or coordinates the insertion point, creating an implicit shared-state conflict that will produce merge conflicts or require one child to re-apply against the other's diff.
findings.suggested_fix: In 44b7's Scope, note 'The failpoint hook must be inserted after 4001's rollback restructure; 44b7 depends on 4001 landing first.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering dependency: 25aa must be scheduled after 1d1b lands (so new shards are recorded in the already-qualified-scope doc).
findings.finding: Children 1d1b-d85f and 25aa-1df0 both modify `docs/mutation-testing.md` without an ordering dependency between them. 1d1b scopes existing scores to '5 cores'; 25aa records new shard scores for concurrency decision logic in the same file. If 25aa lands first, the file still carries the over-broad un-scoped claim that 1d1b is supposed to fix; if they are developed concurrently, merge conflicts are likely on the same prose sections.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'depends_on: [1d1b-d85f]' to 25aa and note in its scope that it writes new shard entries into the already-scoped doc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Coordinate 1fdc and f476 on their shared integration test file: agree on module layout (same file vs separate sibling), fixture namespace, and landing order.
findings.finding: Children 1fdc-f83b and f476-1f56 both add tests to `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` (or a sibling integration module) without cross-referencing each other. 1fdc adds remote-append-during-compaction scenarios; f476 adds same-parent/different-child race tests. Concurrent development on the same file creates implicit shared-state risk (conflicting test names, fixture reuse, ordering of test classes) and merge conflicts.
findings.suggested_fix: Designate one child (e.g., f476, which defines policy) as the owner of the file structure, and have 1fdc add its scenarios in a clearly separated section or a named sibling module such as `test_remote_append_interleaving.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate every child story inline (name + one-line scope) so an executor can create and track them without clarification — e.g., 'Story A: compaction rename fault-injection tests; Story B: compactor process-death recovery; Story C: async-push parent-exit test; Story D: real remote-append interleaving test; Story E: parent-cascade cross-clone race test; Story F: whitespace-title rejection; Story G: docs corrections'.
findings.finding: AC bullet 1 ('All child stories are created, plan-reviewed, and closed') defers all substantive design and scoping decisions to undefined child stories. No child story is enumerated, scoped, or linked in the plan, making this bullet a placeholder that an executor cannot act on without stopping to ask which stories to create and what each must contain.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 1: 'All child stories are created, plan-reviewed, and closed.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'All child stories are created, plan-reviewed, and closed' with a bulleted table mapping each story title to its single-sentence scope (directly in this plan), so the executor has a concrete checklist of stories to open rather than inferring them from the narrative.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Child Stories' section (or enumerated list) to the plan body naming each child story and its scope so the first AC can be verified.
findings.finding: The AC 'All child stories are created, plan-reviewed, and closed' references child stories that are never enumerated or described anywhere in the plan body. No decomposition section, no list of stories, no scope boundaries for individual stories exist, making this criterion unverifiable and leaving the set of child deliverables undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, first bullet: 'All child stories are created, plan-reviewed, and closed.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section such as '## Child Stories' listing each story by title/scope: e.g., (1) Compaction rename fault injection + process death, (2) Async-push parent-exit survival, (3) Real remote-append interleaving, (4) Parent-cascade cross-clone races, (5) Whitespace-only title rejection, (6) Docs corrections. Each entry gives enough scope for the plan-review gate to apply.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC explicitly covering forward-rename fault atomicity (e.g., 'Compaction survives a fault during the forward rename without data loss or corruption') to match the Success Criteria's 'forward- and reverse-rename failure' claim.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria says the compaction fold must be 'all-or-none under forward- and reverse-rename failure', but the corresponding AC only mentions rollback behavior ('Compaction rollback no longer deletes the snapshot after an incomplete rollback') — the forward-rename failure path is not explicitly covered by any AC.
findings.location: Success Criteria paragraph 2 vs. AC bullet 'Compaction rollback no longer deletes the snapshot after an incomplete rollback'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Restate the docs AC to name the three specific claims inline (detached-HEAD claim, flock framing, mutation-doc scope wording) rather than deferring to 'the validation'.
findings.finding: The docs AC defers to an external document ('the stale/over-broad claims identified in the validation') rather than naming the claims inline, while the Success Criteria does name them specifically ('concurrency.md detached-HEAD claim, README flock framing, mutation-doc scope wording'). This terminology inconsistency means the AC's verify step references a different entity than the Success Criteria describes.
findings.location: Success Criteria paragraph 4 vs. AC bullet: 'docs/concurrency.md, README.md, and docs/mutation-testing.md no longer make the stale/over-broad claims identified in the validation.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the docs AC with: 'docs/concurrency.md no longer makes the detached-HEAD claim; README.md no longer frames locking as flock-based; docs/mutation-testing.md no longer uses over-broad scope wording.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or child-story task to document the new all-or-none compaction durability contract (e.g., in an ADR, architecture doc, or inline module docstring) so the guarantee is preserved for future maintainers.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural guarantee — compaction is 'provably all-or-none under forward- and reverse-rename failure' and 'recoverable after real process death' — but schedules no architecture doc or ADR to record this durability contract, leaving the guarantee undocumented for future maintainers.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'The compaction fold is provably all-or-none under forward- and reverse-rename failure, and recoverable after real process death.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that whitespace-title validation is extracted into a single shared utility (e.g., `rebar._validation.validate_title`) invoked by all three facades, so the rule has one source of truth.
findings.finding: The whitespace-only title validation rule must be enforced across three independent facades (CLI, library, MCP) per the AC, but the plan specifies no shared utility or centralized location for the rule. If each facade independently implements `title.strip() == ''`-style checks, future changes to the rejection policy require three coordinated edits, creating silent drift risk.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Whitespace-only titles are rejected across CLI/library/MCP facades'; Context — 'current `if not title` in `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the plan (or the child story for this AC) that a `validate_title(title: str) -> None` helper be introduced in a shared module (e.g., `src/rebar/_validation.py`) and called from the CLI, library, and MCP facades — avoiding triplicated logic and ensuring consistent rejection semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or confirm absence of) existing callers/tests that rely on whitespace-only titles being accepted, and specify how they will be updated or how the fix is validated across all three facades (CLI/library/MCP).
findings.finding: The whitespace-only title rejection is a behavior change to the public CLI/library/MCP API surface, but the plan does not acknowledge whether existing callers (tests, scripts, or integrations) currently pass whitespace-only titles and would break, nor does it specify how those call-sites will be updated or notified.
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria — 'Whitespace-only titles are rejected across CLI/library/MCP facades'; src/rebar/_commands/composer.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the plan confirming a grep/search for whitespace-title usages across the test suite and any documented API examples, and note that the child story for this fix must include updating or adding negative-test cases for all three facades.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit sequencing / dependency notes to the parent plan (or child descriptions) for the compaction children (stampable-basaltine-pony → altruistic-bony-horsefly) and any other order-dependent pairs, so contributors know which children must close first.
findings.finding: The plan provides no sequencing guidance or dependency ordering across the 9 children, even though some children have implicit order dependencies (e.g., the compaction rollback atomicity fix in stampable-basaltine-pony should precede the SIGKILL recovery test in altruistic-bony-horsefly; whitespace validation fix in unwieldy-likeminded-acaciarat should precede the cross-facade corpus test it bundles).
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria / child list
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's child list or a new 'Execution Order' section, annotate: 'altruistic-bony-horsefly depends on stampable-basaltine-pony closing first (code fix must precede recovery test).' Add similar notes for any other order-sensitive pairs identified during child plan-review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale explaining why the chosen rollback mechanism (leaving the SNAPSHOT vs. deleting it) achieves the all-or-none invariant, and how the SNAPSHOT-present idempotency skip in compact_cli behaves safely when some source files remain partially retired after a failed reverse-rename pass.
findings.finding: The plan states the compaction fold is 'provably all-or-none' and names 'forward- and reverse-rename failure' as the two tested axes, but provides no positive rationale for WHY the chosen mechanism (leaving the SNAPSHOT in place on rollback failure) achieves atomicity — specifically, it does not explain how the SNAPSHOT-present early-exit in compact_cli interacts with partially-retired event files, and whether that is safe. Without this rationale, an implementer cannot judge whether the fix is complete or whether an additional recovery probe (e.g., via fsck) is needed.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'The compaction fold is provably all-or-none under forward- and reverse-rename failure, and recoverable after real process death.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'S1/S3/S4' in child 25aa-1df0 with the explicit ticket IDs of the children it depends on.
findings.finding: Child 25aa-1df0 specifies its scheduling dependency as 'AFTER S1/S3/S4 land,' but the sibling roster uses ticket IDs (4001-185c, 44b7-2f9e, a263-515e, etc.) — no child is labeled S1, S3, or S4 anywhere in the plan. The mapping is implicit and unresolvable from plan text alone, creating a residual-reference gap that prevents verification of the dependency.
findings.suggested_fix: In 25aa's AC, change 'scheduled AFTER S1/S3/S4 land' to list the actual ticket IDs (e.g., 4001-185c, 44b7-2f9e, a263-515e, 1fdc-f83b, f476-1f56) and confirm which of those provide the behavioral tests for sync/lock decision logic.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
